notes.anchor: to have played the double game
notes.note: Praevaricatio: collusion between a prosecutor and the defendant he is supposed to be attacking, so as to throw the case; here imputed to the prosecutor L. Lentulus in Gabinius's trial.
Iudicum sordes: a euphemism for bribed or corrupt jurors; the 'filth' of the panel that acquitted Gabinius by the narrow margin of 38 to 32.

notes.anchor: the sap and blood of the state
notes.note: Cicero laments that the res publica has lost not only its vital substance but its very 'colour and form' under the dominance of the dynasts; a recurring elegy for the free republic.
Cicero recalls his consulship of 63 BC, when (as he saw it) he steered the state through the Catilinarian crisis; he contrasts that brief glory with his present marginalization.

notes.anchor: that one man can do everything
notes.note: A guarded reference to the overwhelming power of the dynasts (above all Pompey, backed by Caesar and Crassus); Cicero professes resignation rather than grief.
When no consuls had been elected, patrician senators held the auspices in succession as interreges (each for five days) until elections could be held; a sign of constitutional breakdown.
Rumours that Pompey would be named dictator to resolve the electoral chaos; the office had been dormant since Sulla and aroused deep republican fears.
Ambitus, the bribery of voters, prosecuted under successive statutes; in 54 BC every consular candidate stood accused of it.
A returning promagistrate retained his imperium and was technically immune from prosecution until he crossed the pomerium into the city; P. Sulla argued Gabinius was still outside it.
The lex Papia of 65 BC, which expelled from Rome non-citizens who had usurped citizen status and penalized false claims of citizenship; the freedman Antiochus Gabinius was condemned under it.

notes.anchor: the fourth day before the Nones of November
notes.note: 2 November by the inclusive Roman count; the Nones fell on the fifth of November.
A general needed a valid grant of imperium, ratified by a lex curiata, to qualify for a triumph; the praetors objected that Pomptinus's grant had been irregularly carried.

notes.anchor: the man shall not have his triumph
notes.note: C. Pomptinus, praetor 63 and Cicero's ally against Catiline, had crushed the Allobroges as proconsul of Narbonese Gaul; he finally triumphed in 54 over Cato's bitter objection.

notes.anchor: go off to Cilicia at his own charge, without a law
notes.note: Ap. Claudius Pulcher, consul 54, intended to leave for his province of Cilicia without waiting for the usual enabling law (lex curiata); Cicero would succeed him there in 51.

notes.anchor: the ninth day before the Kalends of November
notes.note: 24 October by the inclusive Roman count; the Kalends fell on the first of November.
M. Pomponius Dionysius, a learned freedman of Atticus who served as tutor to Cicero's son Marcus and nephew Quintus; Cicero longs for his return along with Atticus's.

notes.anchor: the fifth day before the Ides of Sextilis
notes.note: 9 August by the inclusive Roman count. Sextilis was the old name for the month later renamed August in honour of Augustus.
